source_paper_title: Deterministic and Stochastic Frank–Wolfe Recursion on Probability Spaces
context:
Section 2.2 ("Why not simply \"grid and optimize\"?"), page 6

The proposed recursion is not without challenge, however, as it entails solving a global optimization problem during each iterate update. The question of precisely characterizing and comparing the complexity of an a priori finite-dimensionalizing approach such as gridding versus the proposed direct approach is indeed interesting, although we do not undertake this question.
title: Complexity comparison of gridding versus direct Frank--Wolfe optimization over probability measures
latex:
Given a compact set \(\mathcal X\subset\mathbb R^d\) and a functional \(J:\mathcal P(\mathcal X)\to\mathbb R\) on the set \(\mathcal P(\mathcal X)\) of Borel probability measures on \(\mathcal X\), consider the optimization problem
\[
\inf_{\mu\in\mathcal P(\mathcal X)} J(\mu).
\]
Assume \(J\) is accessible either through a deterministic oracle returning \(J(\mu)\) and (when it exists) an influence function \(h_\mu:\mathcal X\to\mathbb R\), or through a Monte Carlo oracle returning unbiased estimates of these quantities.

For \(\Delta>0\), let \(\mathcal L(\Delta)\subset\mathcal X\) be a finite grid of mesh (resolution) \(\Delta\), and let
\[
\mathcal P(\mathcal L(\Delta)):=\{\mu\in\mathcal P(\mathcal X):\operatorname{supp}(\mu)\subseteq\mathcal L(\Delta)\}
\]
be the subset of measures supported on the grid. Let \(\mu^*(\Delta)\in\arg\min_{\mu\in\mathcal P(\mathcal L(\Delta))} J(\mu)\) be an optimal gridded solution.

Also consider a direct (infinite-dimensional) Frank--Wolfe-type method on \(\mathcal P(\mathcal X)\) that, starting from \(\mu_0\in\mathcal P(\mathcal X)\), produces iterates \((\mu_k)_{k\ge 0}\) by
\[
\mu_{k+1}=(1-\eta_k)\mu_k+\eta_k\,\delta_{x_k},\qquad x_k\in\arg\min_{x\in\mathcal X} h_{\mu_k}(x),
\]
where \((\eta_k)_{k\ge 0}\subset(0,1] \) is a stepsize sequence and \(\delta_x\) denotes the Dirac measure at \(x\in\mathcal X\).

Problem: Provide a precise, quantitative comparison (as \(\Delta\downarrow 0\) and as the target accuracy \(\varepsilon\downarrow 0\)) between the computational complexity of (i) solving the gridded problem \(\min_{\mu\in\mathcal P(\mathcal L(\Delta))} J(\mu)\) and (ii) running a direct Frank--Wolfe method on \(\mathcal P(\mathcal X)\), in terms of
- the resulting objective suboptimality gaps \(J(\mu^*(\Delta)) - \inf_{\mu\in\mathcal P(\mathcal X)}J(\mu)\) and \(J(\mu_k)-\inf_{\mu\in\mathcal P(\mathcal X)}J(\mu)\),
- the dependence on dimension \(d\), smoothness/regularity parameters of \(J\), and oracle/noise model assumptions,
- and the total computational cost (e.g., number of oracle evaluations and/or global subproblem solves) required to obtain an \(\varepsilon\)-optimal solution.
location_in_paper: Section 2.2 ("Why not simply \"grid and optimize\"?"), page 6
open_problem_quote: The proposed recursion is not without challenge, however, as it entails solving a global optimization problem during each iterate update. The question of precisely characterizing and comparing the complexity of an a priori finite-dimensionalizing approach such as gridding versus the proposed direct approach is indeed interesting, although we do not undertake this question.
